query: recent studies on cohesin mutations in acute myeloid leukemia

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: Chromosomal translocations: Recent studies have identified new fusion genes in ALL, such as DUX4 rearrangements and KMT2A mutations in infant ALL, highlighting the heterogeneity of this disease. Research is focused on understanding how these fusion genes disrupt gene expression and signaling pathways, driving leukemic transformation. [28] [29] Gene mutations: Gene mutations are a central factor in the development and progression of ALL, with both loss-of-function and gain-of-function mutations contributing to leukemogenesis. Loss-of-function mutations: Recent studies have identified novel tumor suppressor genes involved in ALL, such as TP53 , RB1 , and CREBBP . [20] [30] These mutations disrupt cell cycle regulation, DNA damage repair, and epigenetic control, thereby contributing to uncontrolled cell growth and survival.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: A study even reported that NPM1 mutations were present in up to 35% of patients with AML. [14] Intermediate-risk AML is diagnosed in the presence of any FLT3-ITD mutation or t(9;11)(p21.3;q23.3, or MLL:KMT2A rearrangement). Lastly, high-risk AML categorization can be diagnosed in the presence of several cytogenetic or molecular aberrancies, which notably include monosomy 5/del 5q or 7/deletion 7q, other monosomal or complex karyotype (≥3 unrelated abnormalities), or mutations in ASXL1, EZH2 , SRSF2, or TP53 .

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Cytogenetic Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorescence in situ hybridization: FISH uses fluorescent probes that bind to specific DNA sequences, enabling the detection of chromosomal abnormalities with high sensitivity and specificity. FISH is especially useful for identifying cryptic translocations and gene amplifications. Polymerase chain reaction: PCR-based assays can detect specific gene rearrangements and mutations associated with acute leukemia, providing additional diagnostic and prognostic information. Next-generation sequencing: NGS technologies enable comprehensive analysis of the leukemia genome, identifying a broad range of genetic alterations that may not be detectable by conventional methods. This approach has the potential to revolutionize the diagnosis and treatment of acute leukemia by uncovering novel biomarkers and therapeutic targets.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Clinical Significance -- High Risk of Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: Certain genetic mutations and markers indicate a significantly elevated risk of relapse. For instance, AML patients with complex karyotypes, monosomy 7, or specific translocations such as t(6;9) or t(3;3) are classified as high-risk. [68] Similarly, ALL patients with hypodiploidy or the presence of MLL ( KMT2A ) gene rearrangements face a high risk of relapse. In these cases, aggressive treatment strategies are essential, including the consideration of early HSCT. [60] [79]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Interfering Factors -- Genetic and Epigenetic Heterogeneity
retrieved_docs.content: Acute leukemia is characterized by significant genetic and epigenetic heterogeneity, with different subtypes and clones exhibiting distinct molecular profiles. This heterogeneity can pose challenges in interpreting molecular test results and identifying relevant therapeutic targets. [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Cytogenetic Testing and Conventional Karyotype -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Correct diagnosis, prognostic evaluation, and improved therapeutic options often require appropriate cytogenetic testing in acute leukemias and other forms of cancer. Broader availability of diagnostic tools is key for the advancement of cancer management and survivorship.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Recent techniques, including PCR and flow cytometry, can detect the presence of minimal residual disease in patients with complete remission. Persistently elevated levels of RUNX1 - RUNX1T1 , despite induction therapy, in patients with t(8;21) AML are associated with an increased incidence of relapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment Strategies Based on Genetic Mutations
retrieved_docs.content: TP53 mutations: TP53 mutations are associated with poor prognosis and resistance to standard chemotherapy in both AML and ALL. Treatment strategies for patients with TP53 mutations may include the use of hypomethylating agents (eg, azacitidine and decitabine) or participation in clinical trials that explore novel therapeutic approaches. [78]

retrieved_docs.title: Myelodysplastic Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: MDS is a clonal disorder of myeloid stem cells that may occur de novo or secondary to various insults to the bone marrow. Various environmental and iatrogenic etiologies have been implicated in MDS, including exposure to chemotherapy (alkylating agents in particular), radiation, or environmental toxins such as benzene. Familial MDS has been reported but is a rare entity. [1] The actual preceding factor(s) for de novo MDS is not entirely understood but assumed to occur from an oncogenic process resulting in 1 or more somatic mutations. Over recent years, we have gained much insight into mutations commonly altered in MDS due to advances and rapid availability of gene sequencing. With these developments, researchers can identify 1 or more driver mutations in up to 80% to 90% of patients with some of the most common mutations, including SF3B1, TET2, SRSF2, ASXL1, DNMT3A, RUNX1, U2AF1, TP53 , and EZH2. RUNX1, for example, is a mutation noted to disrupt normal hematopoiesis. More than 100 genes are recurrently mutated in MDS, and these encode spliceosome components, chromatin remodeling factors, epigenetic pattern modulators, and transcription factors. [2] These driver mutations have been found to correlate with different clinical features, including the severity of cytopenias, blast percentage, cytogenetics, and overall survival. Of note, genetic mutations are not included in prognostic scoring systems for MDS, but they have been found to influence overall survival in some cases. TP53 , for example, is a tumor suppressor gene with a poor prognosis compared to other mutations. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Myeloid Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of AML is multifaceted and complex, driven by a multitude of genetic and epigenetic alterations. These include chromosomal abnormalities such as translocations, inversions, and deletions, as well as mutations in genes regulating myeloid cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival. These specific genetic alterations are critical in determining the disease's aggressiveness, response to treatment, and prognosis. [32] Broadly, these alterations can be classified into 2 categories based on their functional consequences, as outlined below.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia With Variant Genetics -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The second most common chromosomal abnormality, Trisomy 12, is found in approximately 10 to 20% of CLL tumors and is involved in the upregulation of the gene RUNX3. [23] Currently, the involvement of the RUNX3 gene and the overall impact of Trisomy 12 on CLL remains unclear and is a topic of further research. [23] Del(11q22-23) has also been found in approximately 10 to 20% of CLL tumors. [24] This deleted region has the ataxia-telangiectasia (ATM) gene, which is responsible for detecting damaged DNA, which plays a crucial function in the normal cell cycle and, ultimately, cell apoptosis. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (Nursing) -- Causes
retrieved_docs.content: In 2016, World Health Organization (WHO) revised the classification and categorized it into AML with recurrent genetic abnormalities, AML with myelodysplasia-related changes, therapy-related myeloid neoplasms, NOS, myeloid sarcoma, myeloid proliferations related to Down syndrome. AML with recurrent genetic abnormalities includes- AML with t(8;21)(q22;q22.1); RUNX1-RUNX1T1 AML with inv(16)(p13.1q22) or t(16;16)(p13.1;q22); CBFB-MYH11 APL with t(15;17)(q22;q12); PML-RARA AML with t(9;11)(p21.3;q23.3); MLLT3-KMT2A AML with t(6;9)(p23;q24); DEK-NUP214 AML with inv(3)(q21.3q26.2) or t(3;3)(q21.3;q26.2); GATA2, MECOM AML (megakaryoblastic) with t(1;22)(p13.3;q13.3); RBM15-MKL1 AML with mutated NPM1 AML with biallelic mutations CEBPA AML not otherwise specified (NOS) includes:

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Genetic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic testing methods: Genetic testing in acute leukemia is essential for detecting specific genetic alterations that inform diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment decisions. Several techniques are used, each offering unique advantages. Polymerase chain reaction: PCR is a molecular technique used to amplify specific DNA sequences, allowing for the detection of genetic abnormalities such as gene rearrangements, mutations, and translocations. PCR is highly sensitive and specific, making it a valuable tool for identifying MRD and monitoring treatment response. [56] Fluorescence in situ hybridization: FISH utilizes fluorescent probes that bind to specific DNA sequences, enabling the visualization of chromosomal abnormalities and gene fusions in interphase cells or metaphase chromosomes. FISH is commonly used to detect recurrent translocations, such as t(9;22) ( BCR-ABL1 ) and t(12;21) ( ETV6-RUNX1 ), which are associated with specific leukemia subtypes. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Cytogenetic Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: Role of cytogenetic analysis in diagnosis: Cytogenetic analysis is essential for the accurate diagnosis and classification of acute leukemia. Specific chromosomal abnormalities are often associated with distinct subtypes of the disease. Acute lymphoblastic leukemia: Common cytogenetic abnormalities in ALL include the t(9;22) translocation, also known as the Philadelphia chromosome, and the t(12;21) translocation involving the ETV6 and RUNX1 genes. [48] Acute myeloid leukemia: Recurrent cytogenetic abnormalities in AML include the t(8;21) translocation, inv(16), and t(15;17) translocation, each associated with distinct morphological and clinical features. These chromosomal abnormalities not only aid in the diagnosis but also have prognostic implications, guiding risk-adapted treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Acute Leukemia -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Myeloid Leukemia
retrieved_docs.content: Class II mutations: Recent studies have identified novel mutations in transcription factors such as KMT2A and NUP98 , which disrupt normal hematopoietic differentiation and contribute to AML pathogenesis. These mutations alter gene expression patterns and impair myeloid cell maturation, leading to the accumulation of immature blasts. [33] [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Cytogenetic Testing and Conventional Karyotype -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) detects chromosome abnormalities targeting specific genetic sequences using probes with a fluorescent dye that attaches to complementary DNA. It can be performed in the interphase stage, but it does not analyze the full set of chromosomes, only the sequences targeted by the utilized probes. Therefore, a comprehensive analysis of the case with an adequate differential diagnosis is mandatory before selecting specific probes. FISH studies can detect inversions, insertions, translocations, microdeletions, and microduplications. [3] [4] An example of FISH analysis is the detection of inv(16)(p13q22), involving the fusion gene CBFB-MYH11, in acute myeloid leukemia (AML). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor, meaning it belongs to the class of drugs that specifically target BCR‑ABL fusion proteins. BCR‑ABL fusion proteins serve as the molecular target for BCR‑ABL inhibitors; they are oncogenic drivers created by the Philadelphia chromosome translocation and are central to the pathogenesis of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By binding to and inhibiting these fusion proteins, imatinib and other BCR‑ABL inhibitors can suppress the abnormal signaling that promotes uncontrolled cell proliferation, thereby providing an effective targeted therapy for patients with these malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Gilteritinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), offering a potential option for patients with this aggressive hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and dasatinib anhydrous are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a class of targeted therapies that block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase activity implicated in certain leukemias. By inhibiting this oncogenic kinase, both the standard and anhydrous formulations of dasatinib help to control malignant cell proliferation and are used in the management of chronic myeloid leukemia and Philadelphia chromosome‑positive acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Recognizing dasatinib and its anhydrous form as BCR‑ABL inhibitors underscores their shared mechanism of action and therapeutic role in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2917
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and imatinib mesylát are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a form of lymphoid leukemia. Both drugs target abnormal tyrosine‑kinase activity characteristic of this hematologic malignancy, providing a targeted approach to disease control and highlighting the role of kinase inhibitors in modern leukemia treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1437
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib, also known as imatinib mesylate, is a BCR‑ABL inhibitor; it belongs to the class of agents that target the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase and is used therapeutically to inhibit this oncogenic driver in relevant hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
retrieved_docs.content: support to immune‑modulating virotherapy—used for the treatment of hematologic malignancies such as leukemia and other cancers, while also identifying specific contraindications and adjunctive uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are both classified as BCR‑ABL inhibitors. As BCR‑ABL inhibitors, these agents specifically target the BCR‑ABL fusion protein, a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives the development of chronic myeloid leukemia and certain acute lymphoblastic leukemias. By inhibiting BCR‑ABL activity, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib disrupt downstream signaling pathways, reducing malignant cell proliferation and providing therapeutic benefit in BCR‑ABL–positive hematologic malignancies. Their shared classification highlights a common mechanism of action, while differences in potency, spectrum of activity, and side‑effect profiles inform clinicians’ choices of targeted therapy for patients with BCR‑ABL–driven disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 329
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” comprises the kinase‑targeted agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib and midostaurin. Both gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated as potential therapies for acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). As members of the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, these drugs share a common mechanism of action that involves inhibition of specific signaling pathways driving malignant cell proliferation, and their use in AML highlights the therapeutic relevance of kinase inhibition in hematologic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1768
retrieved_docs.content: BCR-ABL Inhibitor is a class of targeted therapy that includes ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib. Ponatinib and its hydrochloride form, as well as asciminib, are members of the BCR-ABL Inhibitor class and are employed in the treatment of أبيضاض الدم النقوي, a myeloid leukemia. These agents act by inhibiting the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase, thereby controlling disease progression. Their shared mechanism and therapeutic indication underscore the importance of BCR‑ABL inhibition in managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: Midostaurin is a therapeutic agent that may be used to treat acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1287
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and its anhydrous form, dasatinib anhydrous, are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for treating ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, indicating that this tyrosine‑kinase inhibitor and its dehydrated variant may be used in the management of this lymphoid leukemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1289
retrieved_docs.content: Ibrutinib is a targeted therapeutic agent that may be used to treat سرطـان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia), offering a pharmacologic option for managing this hematologic malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 326
retrieved_docs.content: Ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are therapeutic agents that may be used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي). Both ponatinib and its hydrochloride salt share the same indication, reflecting that the active compound is effective in this hematologic malignancy. Asciminib, a distinct BCR‑ABL1 inhibitor, also offers a treatment option for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, particularly those with resistance to earlier tyrosine‑kinase inhibitors. The availability of multiple agents targeting the same disease provides clinicians with alternative strategies to achieve disease control and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1504
retrieved_docs.content: Dasatinib and imatinib are both tyrosine‑kinase inhibitor medications that may be used to treat ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي, a form of lymphoid leukemia. Both agents target abnormal signaling pathways in malignant lymphoid cells, offering therapeutic options for patients with this hematologic malignancy. Their use reflects the importance of targeted therapy in managing lymphoid leukemias and underscores the need for individualized treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2697
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “cancer; other and unspecified primary” includes malignant blood tumors (الأورام الدموية الخبيثة (SMQ)) as a member of this group, indicating that these hematologic malignancies are categorized under cancers of other or unspecified primary origin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2801
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Biomarker Terminology is a specialized classification system that incorporates the concept of bcr-abl fusion proteins as well as the concept of glukokortikoidy (glucocorticoids). By including bcr-abl fusion proteins, the terminology acknowledges the relevance of these oncogenic fusion proteins—commonly associated with chronic myeloid leukemia and other hematologic malignancies—as important biomarkers for disease detection, prognosis, and targeted therapy. The inclusion of glukokortikoidy reflects the significance of glucocorticoids, a class of steroid hormones that are widely used in clinical practice for their anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects and can also serve as biomarkers for therapeutic response and endocrine function. Together, these concepts illustrate the breadth of CTRP Biomarker Terminology in covering both molecular oncogenic markers and pharmacologically active hormone classes relevant to patient diagnosis and management.
